RUVprps normalization on TCGA BRCA RNA-seq data - data preprocessing

Momeneh Foroutan1 and Ramyar Molania2,3

1Biomedicine Discovery Institute and the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Monash University, VIC, Australia.
2Department of Medical Oncology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Boston, MA, USA
3Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA.

2026-06-02

Package

RUVprps 0.99.0

1 Introduction

We show the application of the RUVprps package to the TCGA Breast Cancer Invasive Carcinoma (BRCA) RNA-seq dataset. The goal is to remove the variation associated with tumor purity, alongside other sources of unwanted variation (UV) such as library size and batch effects, using the RUV-III with Pseudo-Replicates of Pseudo-Samples (PRPS) framework. In this, we show how to preprocess the TCGA BRCA RNA-seq data for RUV-III with PRPS normalization.

1.1 Overview of the pipeline

The RUVprps procedure consists of four stages, each with one or more steps:

Stage Purpose
1 Data preparation and processing
2 Identification of biological variation and UV
3 Variation assessment
4 RUV-III normalization and performance evaluation

1.2 Required packages

The R packages below are required for the analysis in this vignette.

library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(ComplexHeatmap)
library(TCGAbiolinks)
library(gridExtra)
library(ggplot2)
library(RUVprps)
library(genefu)
library(ggpubr)
library(dplyr)
library(grid)
library(qs)

2 Data preparation and processing

2.1 Step 1: downloading the data

The most recent TCGA BRCA RNA-seq data are downloaded from the GDC data portal using the TCGAbiolinks R package. The files are saved to the current working directory.After preparation, normal tissue samples are excluded so that only primary tumor samples are retained for all downstream analyses.

tcga.brca.rnaseq.data <- GDCquery(
    project       = 'TCGA-BRCA',
    data.category = 'Transcriptome Profiling',
    data.type     = 'Gene Expression Quantification')
GDCdownload(tcga.brca.rnaseq.data)
brca.se.obj <- GDCprepare(query = tcga.brca.rnaseq.data)
# Retain only primary tumor tissue samples.
brca.se.obj <- brca.se.obj[, brca.se.obj$tissue_type == 'Tumor']
# we save this object for reproduciblity
# qs::qsave(
#     x = brca.se.obj, 
#     file = 'TCGA_BrcaRnaSeq_StarCounts38_TCGAbiolinks.021424.qs'
#     )

The resulting brca.se.obj is a RangedSummarizedExperiment with 60,660 Ensembl transcripts and 1,231 RNA-seq samples (1,083 tumour + 113 normal before filtering). It contains six datasets (assays):

Data (assay) Description
unstranded Raw counts, unstranded (used as RawCount)
stranded_first Raw counts, first-strand
stranded_second Raw counts, second-strand
tpm_unstrand TPM normalized
fpkm_unstrand FPKM normalized
fpkm_uq_unstrand FPKM normalized followed by upper-quartile normalization

Only the unstranded assay is retained as the raw count data, because the TCGA-provided normalization (TPM, FPKM, FPKM.UQ) were all derived from the unstranded counts. We save the brca.se.obj for reproducibility and it can be found on the XXX.

brca.se.obj <- qread(file = 'TCGA_BrcaRnaSeq_StarCounts38_TCGAbiolinks.021424.qs')
brca.se.obj <- brca.se.obj[ , brca.se.obj$tissue_type == 'Tumor']
# removeAssays() drops the two stranded count assays that are redundant with
# the unstranded assay for this analysis. 
brca.se.obj <- removeAssays(
    se.obj           = brca.se.obj,
    assays.to.remove = c('stranded_first', 'stranded_second'))
# renameAssays() replaces the original TCGA assay names with shorter or other names,
brca.se.obj <- renameAssays(
    se.obj    = brca.se.obj,
    new.names = c('RawCount', 'TPM', 'FPKM', 'FPKM.UQ'))

2.2 Step 2: Selecting gene types of interest (optional)

The gene_type column in the gene annotation (rowData) classifies each Ensembl transcript into a biotype category (e.g. protein_coding, lncRNA, pseudogene, …). For this analysis only protein-coding genes with unique HGNC symbols are retained. Row names of the SummarizedExperiment are updated from Ensembl IDs to HGNC gene symbols to simplify downstream interpretation and to enable matching against external gene lists (e.g. housekeeping genes, PAM50 signature genes).

# tidyGenes() performs three operations in one call:
#   1. Retains only rows annotated as 'protein_coding' in gene_type.
#   2. Removes duplicate HGNC symbols (keeping one representative transcript
#      per gene symbol) so that row names are unique.
#   3. Replaces Ensembl IDs with HGNC symbols as row names
#      (change.row.names = TRUE, new.row.names = 'gene_name').
brca.se.obj <- tidyGenes(
    se.obj                = brca.se.obj,
    keep.gene.type        = 'protein_coding',
    gene.type.col.name    = 'gene_type',
    remove.duplicates.ids = TRUE,
    ids.col.name          = 'gene_name',
    change.row.names      = TRUE,
    new.row.names         = 'gene_name')
rowData(brca.se.obj)

After filtering, 19,934 Ensembl transcripts are retained.

2.3 Step 3: Adding batch details

TCGA RNA-seq samples were collected from multiple Tissue Source Sites (TSS), shipped to different genomics profiling centers, and sequenced on 96-well sequencing plates across several years. These are well-documented sources of batch effects that affect downstream analyses.

addTcgaBatchInfo() annotates each sample with all available TCGA batch metadata, retrieved from the supplementary file 41587_2022_1440_MOESM3_ESM.xlsx from Molania et al. ( Nature Biotechnology2023). The TCGA sample barcode encodes TSS, plate, and centre information (see the GDC barcode documentation for full details).

orderSeObj() then sorts samples chronologically by the specified batch variables so that time-related batch patterns become contiguous, a requirement for the RLE-based batch detection used in Stage 2.

# addTcgaBatchInfo() parses the TCGA sample barcodes and the external
# batch annotation file to append Year, Month, Day, Plate, TSS, and
# Center information to colData as new columns.
brca.se.obj       <- addTcgaBatchInfo(se.obj = brca.se.obj)
brca.se.obj$Years <- as.factor(x = brca.se.obj$Years)

# orderSeObj() sorts samples first by sequencing year, then by plate ID.
brca.se.obj <- orderSeObj(
    se.obj           = brca.se.obj,
    factors.to.order = c('Years', 'Plates'))

The TCGA BRCA RNA-seq dataset now comprises 1,083 cancer tissue samples collected from 40 Tissue Source Sites (TSS), distributed across 37 sequencing plates, and profiled over 5 years (2010–2014). Samples from 2010–2011 were sequenced using one flow cell chemistry; samples from 2012–2014 used a different chemistry. This chemistry change is a major source of UV in this dataset.

2.4 Step 4: Preparing the SummarizedExperiment object

prepareSeObj() performs several preprocessing tasks in a single call, producing a fully annotated object ready for normalization:

  • Lowly expressed gene removal: genes failing the CPM expression cutoff in a minimum number of samples are dropped.
  • Library size calculation: per-sample total count sums are computed from the RawCount assay and stored in colData.
  • Tumor purity estimation: two methods are applied, the ESTIMATE and singscore methods, both computed from the FPKM.UQ assay. The singscore values are rescaled to the ESTIMATE [0, 1].
  • Housekeeping and immune/stromal gene integration: curated gene lists are attached to rowData for use as negative control gene (NCG) candidates and biological reference sets.

The column.name = 'gene_name' argument tells the function which rowData column contains the gene identifiers; it uses this to create a new hgnc_symbol column for gene-list matching.

brca.se.obj <- prepareSeObj(
    data                          = brca.se.obj,
    raw.count.assay.name          = 'RawCount',
    remove.lowly.expressed.genes  = TRUE,
    assay.name.to.estimate.purity = 'FPKM.UQ',
    estimate.tumor.purity         = 'both',
    scale.singscore.values        = TRUE,
    calculate.library.size        = TRUE,
    add.immun.stroma.genes        = TRUE,
    add.gene.details              = TRUE,
    add.housekeeping.genes        = TRUE,
    column.name                   = 'gene_name',
    gene.group                    = 'hgnc_symbol', 
    verbose                       = FALSE)
# qs::qsave(
#     x    = brca.se.obj, 
#     file = 'TCGA_BrcaRnaSeq_StarCounts38_TCGAbiolinks.021424_RuvSeObj.qs')

15,304 of 19,934 Ensembl transcripts pass the expression filter (CPM \(\geq\) 0.18 in at least 598 samples — the default threshold of the function). Tumor purity estimated by ESTIMATE and singscore are highly correlated; the ESTIMATE values are used for all downstream analysis.

2.5 Step 5: Visualising the study outline

plotStudyOutline() produces a summary figure showing the distribution of all specified variables across samples and a pairwise correlation matrix between them. Before plotting, variable names are standardized with renameVariables().

# renameVariables() changes three internal colData column names to the
# labels used consistently throughout the rest of this vignette
brca.se.obj <- renameVariables(
    se.obj        = brca.se.obj,
    current.names = c('tissue_type', 'tumour.purity.estimate', 'library.size'),
    new.names     = c('Tissues', 'Tumour.purity', 'Library.size'))

# plotStudyOutline() generates:
#   - A heatmap showing the distribution of each variable across samples
#     (ordered by the batch variables applied in addTcgaBatchInfo / orderSeObj)
#   - A pairwise correlation matrix across all specified variables
brca.se.obj <- plotStudyOutline(
    se.obj      = brca.se.obj, 
    plot.output = FALSE, 
    variables   = c('Years', 'Plates', 'TSS', 'Library.size', 'Tumour.purity'),
    verbose     = FALSE)
grid.arrange(
  grid.grabExpr(draw(brca.se.obj@metadata$StudyOutline)),
  grid.grabExpr(draw(brca.se.obj@metadata$VariablesAssociation)),
  ncol = 1
  )

Figure 1: Study outline of the TCGA BRCA RNA-seq data
The heatmap shows that the data were collected from 40 tissue source sites (TSS) and distributed across 40 sequencing plates for profiling at 5 time points over a span of 5 years. The library sizes were calculated using the raw counts after removing lowly expressed genes. Tumor purity was estimated using the ESTIMATE method on the TCGA FPKM.UQ normalized data. Correlation matrix show association between the variables.

Plot below shows that correlation between the tumor purity estimated using the ESTIMATE and singscore methods

purity.plot <- data.frame(
    estimate  = brca.se.obj$Tumour.purity, 
    singscore = brca.se.obj$tumour.purity.singscore.scaled
    )
ggplot(purity.plot, aes(x = estimate, y = singscore)) +
    geom_point() +
    xlab('Estimate') +
    ylab('singscore (scaled)') +
    stat_cor(size = 5) +
    theme(
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        axis.line        = element_line(colour = 'black', linewidth = 1),
        axis.title.x     = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.title.y     = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x      = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text.y      = element_text(size = 14))

Figure 2: This scatter plot shows the correlation between tumor purity obtained using the ESTIMATE and singscore methods

Tumour purity estimates from ESTIMATE and singscore are highly correlated (Figure 2); ESTIMATE values are used for all downstream analysis.

The plotStudyOutline() output reveals that library sizes vary systematically across samples: samples profiled between 2012–2014 show lower library sizes than 2010–2011 samples (Figure 2a). This difference is attributable to the change in flow cell chemistry between the two periods . Accordingly, samples are divided into two major batches:

# A binary batch label is created to explicitly encode the flow cell chemistry
# change. Samples from 2010–2011 are assigned to Batch1 (older chemistry);
# samples from 2012–2014 are assigned to Batch2 (newer chemistry).
brca.se.obj$Flow.cells <- factor(
    ifelse(brca.se.obj$Years %in% c(2010, 2011), "Batch1", "Batch2"),
    levels = c("Batch1", "Batch2"))

3 Identification of Major Variation

Note: This stage is unnecessary when all sources of unwanted variation and the primary gene-expression-based biological variation are already known. Only biological variables that are detectable in the expression data can be used effectively in the RUV-III normalisation procedure.

3.1 Step 1: Estimating PAM50 biological populations

Breast cancers are classified into five intrinsic molecular subtypes based on the PAM50 50-gene signature: Basal, Her2, Luminal A (LumA), Luminal B (LumB), and Normal-like. These subtypes are strongly associated with clinical outcomes — Basal and Her2 subtypes carry the worst prognosis, while LumA is associated with the best survival.

The TCGA publication provides PAM50 annotations in the paper_BRCA_Subtype_PAM50 column of the SummarizedExperiment; these are stored as UCF.PAM50. Additionally, estimatePAM50() applies the genefu algorithm to estimate PAM50 subtypes de novo from each normalised assay.

# Store the TCGA publication PAM50 annotations under a standardised name.
# "Normal" (normal-like tumour subtype) is relabelled to "Normal.like" to
# avoid confusion with normal tissue samples.
# NA entries (samples not classified in the TCGA paper) become "Not.classified"
# so they can be easily excluded from survival analysis without dropping rows.
brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50 <- brca.se.obj$paper_BRCA_Subtype_PAM50
brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50[brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50 == 'Normal'] <- 'Normal.like'
brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50[is.na(brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50)]      <- 'Not.classified'
brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50 <- factor(
    x      = brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50,
    levels = c('Basal', 'Her2', 'LumA', 'LumB', 'Normal.like', 'Not.classified'))

# estimatePAM50() applies the genefu implementation for PAM50 estimates
brca.se.obj <- estimatePAM50(
    se.obj         = brca.se.obj,
    assay.names    = 'all',
    entrez.gene.id = 'entrezgene_id',
    probe          = 'gene_name',
    normalization  = NULL,
    tissue.type    = NULL, 
    apply.log      = TRUE,
    verbose        = FALSE)

3.2 Step 2: Evaluating PAM50 subtype quality

Two analyses assess the quality of each assay’s PAM50 estimates:

  1. Survival association. a well-annotated PAM50 estimate should show a significant log-rank p-value in overall survival analysis.
  2. Library size association. an ideal PAM50 estimate should show no or weak association with library size, which is a purely technical variable.

3.2.1 Survival analysis

addTcgaClinicalInfo() integrates TCGA clinical data from Liu et al. (2018) (available as Supplementary Table 1 “mmc1.xlsx”) into the SummarizedExperiment. Normal tissue samples receive a value of −1 for survival time and event, enabling their automatic exclusion from survival computations. computeSurvival() then generates Kaplan–Meier curves for each PAM50 classification.

# addTcgaClinicalInfo() appends the overall survival time (OS.time, in days)
# and survival event indicator (OS: 1 = deceased, 0 = censored) to colData.
# Normal tissue samples are assigned OS = -1 so that
# the idx filter below can exclude them without additional subsetting.
brca.se.obj <- addTcgaClinicalInfo(
    se.obj      = brca.se.obj,
    factors     = c("OS", "OS.time"),
    tissue.type = 'Tissues')

# Apply survival analysis to every PAM50 column simultaneously using lapply.
# grep('\\.PAM50$', ...) identifies all columns ending in ".PAM50" in colData,
# which includes both the TCGA annotation (UCF.PAM50) and the per-assay
# genefu estimates (e.g. FPKM.UQ.PAM50).
# idx excludes normal tissue samples (OS == -1) and unclassified samples
# ('Not.classified') from the Kaplan–Meier computation.
all.survivals <- lapply(
    pam50 <- grep('\\.PAM50$', colnames(colData(brca.se.obj)), value = TRUE)[c(2,3,4,5,1)],
    function(i) {
        idx <- brca.se.obj$OS != -1 & brca.se.obj[[i]] != 'Not.classified'
        computeSurvival(
            se.obj             = brca.se.obj[, idx],
            variable           = i,
            survival.time      = 'OS.time',
            survival.events    = 'OS',
            genes              = NULL,
            return.survival.plot = TRUE,
            check.se.obj       = FALSE,
            plot.output        = FALSE, 
            verbose            = FALSE,
            save.se.obj        = FALSE)$plot
    })

ggarrange(
    plotlist = all.survivals, 
    ncol = 2, 
    nrow = 3, 
    common.legend = TRUE
    )

Figure 3: Kaplan-Meier plots showing the association between the estimated PAM50 subtypes and the survival outcomes of patients for different datasets

The survival analysis shows that the FPKM.UQ-derived PAM50 estimates are the most accurate (lowest log-rank p-value; Figure 3), reflecting the fact that FPKM.UQ partially corrects for library size variation before subtype estimation. These estimates are used as the primary known biological variation for all downstream RUV-III normalisation steps.

3.2.2 Association with library size

The plotVariables() function produces boxplots of library size stratified by PAM50 subtype for each assay. An ideal PAM50 estimate should show no association with library size (a purely technical variable). Significant association suggests that the subtype estimates are confounded by technical effects.

# plotVariables() creates one boxplot panel per PAM50 column (x.variables),
# showing the distribution of Library.size (y.variable) within each subtype.
# generate.heatmap = TRUE also produces a summary heatmap of p-values from
# one-way ANOVA tests across all variable pairs.
# Not.classified samples are removed before plotting to focus on the five
# biologically interpretable subtypes.
to.plot <- plotVariables(
    se.obj        = brca.se.obj[, brca.se.obj$UCF.PAM50 != 'Not.classified'],
    x.variables   = grep('\\.PAM50$',
                       colnames(colData(brca.se.obj)),
                       value = TRUE)[c(2, 3, 4, 5, 1)],
    y.variables  = 'Library.size',
    y.lab        = expression(Log[2] ~ library ~ size),
    generate.heatmap = TRUE,
    nb.ncol      = 2, 
    plot.out.put = FALSE)
to.plot$p.x.y

Figure 4: Shows the distribution of library size within each estimated PAM50
One-way ANOVA was used to assess the association.

Figure 4 shows that PAM50 subtypes are significantly associated with library size across all assays, indicating that subtype estimates may be partially confounded by technical variation. The FPKM.UQ-derived PAM50 shows the weakest association, consistent with its stronger survival signal. Figure 5 shows no substantial differences in PAM50 assignments across the different assays.

We save the brca.se.obj object for RUV-III normalization analysis.

# saving the the `brca.se.obj` object
qs::qsave(x = brca.se.obj, 'TCGA_BRCA_PreProcssed.qs')

4 Summary of the analyses

The table below summarises the key objects and annotations produced during Stages 1 and 2, which are the inputs to the variation assessment (Stage 3) and normalisation (Stage 4) steps.

Object / annotation Description
brca.se.obj SummarizedExperiment with 15,304 genes × 1,083 tumour samples
Assays RawCount, TPM, FPKM, FPKM.UQ
colData$Years Sequencing year (2010–2014), factor
colData$Plates Sequencing plate ID
colData$TSS Tissue Source Site code
colData$Flow.cells Flow cell chemistry batch (Batch1 / Batch2)
colData$Tumour.purity ESTIMATE tumour purity [0, 1]
colData$Library.size Log₂ total counts per sample
colData$UCF.PAM50 TCGA-paper PAM50 annotations
colData$FPKM.UQ.PAM50 genefu PAM50 on FPKM.UQ (primary biological variable)

5 Session Information

The out put of sessionInfo() can be found on the GitHub.
